*Projection based on 2023 prison population. Includes people in prison with sentences more than one year who have not already been released on parole and excludes people with life sentences.

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Often, there is a minimum term a person must serve before becoming parole eligible. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In New York, most people who are incarcerated for violent, sex, and drug felony offenses are not eligible for discretionary parole, although their sentence can be reduced roughly 14 percent by “good time.” All other people serving a sentence for an offense that carries a judicial maximum of at least three years but not life are eligible for discretionary parole after a minimum set by the court in a range between one year to one third of the maximum sentence set by the court. The minimum can be reduced by “good time” credits.²
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility Most recent data shows that 40 percent of people in prison were eligible for parole and incarcerated past parole eligibility at the end of the year, while another 51 were expected to reach their parole eligibility in the following year. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 6 percent. Our forecasting model projects that the percentage of people past their initial parole eligibility will remain around 40 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 51 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 97 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 40 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 60 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense types among people in prison past parole eligibility were public order offenses (23 percent) and property offenses (23 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 41 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 5 and 9.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.³ (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.⁴ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 1 year when the most serious offense is non-violent and for people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was shorter. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁵
## Endnotes ¹ Alexis Lee Watts, Kevin R. Reitz, and Michael McBride, Profiles in Parole Release and Revocation: Examining the Legal Framework in the United States – New York (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, 2016), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/new_york_parole_profile_web_final.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-02/new_york_parole_profile_web_final.pdf), 12.

New York State Department of Corrections and Community Supervision. “About the Board.” Accessed November 12, 2024. [https://doccs.ny.gov/about-board](https://doccs.ny.gov/about-board).

² Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: New York (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, December 2020), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/publications/prison-release-discretion-and-prison-population-size-state-reports), 6-9.


³ US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, [https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38](https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38).

⁴ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf), 36-43.

⁵ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, [https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.The](https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.The) Assembly State of New York, 2023 Annual Report (Albany, NY: Committee on Correction, The Assembly State of New York, 2023), accessed October 1, 2024, [https://nyassembly.gov/write/upload/postings/2024/pdfs/20240318_0109548.pdf](https://nyassembly.gov/write/upload/postings/2024/pdfs/20240318_0109548.pdf), 1. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 46 percent, changing from 56,656 in 2010 to 30,338 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 49 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 96 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 56 percent of people in prison were in prison for violent offenses and 44 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people in prison were murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses (17 percent). ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths 28 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 5 and 9.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, the number of people released from prison decreased 37 percent, from 25,560 in 2010 to 16,216 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In the most recent year of data available, 77 percent of parole-eligible people released in New York were released past their parole eligibility year, which is a decrease of 5 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. 98 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity 45 percent of people released from prison were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex 93 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age 37 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types 35 percent of people released from prison were in prison for violent offenses and 65 percent for nonviolent offenses. The most common offense type among people released from prison were property offenses (26 percent). ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Parole Release ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_black_new_york.png){width=1177} ```{=html} Black, non-Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in New York.

``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_new_york.png){width=1180} ```{=html} Hispanic people were 10 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility compared to White people, when accounting for population sizes in New York.
Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.
```

National Corrections Reporting Program, 2019 and CSG Justice Center Estimates

``` ::: ::: {.cell} :::
## Notes on Disparities **Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes** In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex. The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility. **Metrics Displayed in Visualizations** Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex: - **RRI of Parole Release:** This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole. - **Average Time Past Parole Eligibility:** For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups. - **Average Total Time Served:** We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities. **Data Considerations** Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology. Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states: - Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations. - Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals. - Including 'Other' Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis. **Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation** Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to the NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis. ::: --- title: "New York" ---
